skill.name: routeros-firewall
skill.description: RouterOS firewall filter, NAT, mangle, and address-list configuration. Use when: writing firewall rules in RouterOS, configuring NAT, setting up address-lists or interface-lists, writing idempotent firewall scripts, configuring DNS redirect or port forwarding, or when the user mentions /ip/firewall, chain=forward, chain=input, connection-state, address-list, interface-list, or layer7-protocol on MikroTik.
skill.summary: RouterOS firewall filter, NAT, mangle, and address-list configuration skill for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code agents.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: All 144 static scanner findings are false positives. The skill is pure RouterOS firewall configuration documentation (Markdown .md files) containing CLI syntax examples. No executable code, no external command execution, no credential exfiltration. Hardcoded IPs/URLs are legitimate documentation examples (private CIDR ranges, MikroTik docs). The skill teaches firewall configuration best practices.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Flag (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static scanner incorrectly flagged YAML frontmatter and documentation text mentioning 'psd' (Port Scan Detection) as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are RouterOS firewall configuration documentation entries, not cryptographic implementations.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md:3 is YAML frontmatter description field. dos-protection.md:7 describes the psd (Port Scan Detection) matcher - a legitimate RouterOS firewall feature, not cryptography.

high_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Flag (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static scanner incorrectly flagged connection-state firewall rules as system reconnaissance. These are valid RouterOS connection tracking patterns for firewall configuration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md:82 shows 'connection-state=invalid action=drop' - legitimate firewall rule. SKILL.md:132 shows mistake example in a table, not actual reconnaissance code.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution Flags (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 123 occurrences of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but these are Markdown documentation files containing RouterOS CLI syntax examples wrapped in backticks. These are NOT executable Ruby scripts - they are documentation showing command syntax like /ip/firewall/filter/add.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All three files are .md documentation files with RouterOS configuration examples. Backticks delimit code syntax, not executable commands. The content is educational firewall configuration documentation.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Address Flags (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 16 hardcoded IP addresses, but all are legitimate documentation examples using private CIDR ranges (10.0.0.0/8, 192.168.0.0/16) and example subnets (10.20.0.0/24). This is standard practice for network documentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All IP addresses are documentation examples showing RouterOS configuration patterns. No actual hardcoded production IPs are present.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Flags (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged URLs to MikroTik official documentation (help.mikrotik.com). These are legitimate reference links to vendor documentation included as best practice for skill documentation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to MikroTik official help documentation. Including vendor documentation links is appropriate for a RouterOS skill.

content.user_title: Configure RouterOS Firewall Rules with AI
content.value_statement: Writing RouterOS firewall rules manually leads to misconfiguration and security gaps. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code generate correct firewall configurations including filter rules, NAT, mangle chains, address-lists, and DoS protection patterns.
actual_capabilities: Generate RouterOS firewall filter rules with correct chain, action, and placement
Configure NAT port forwarding and DNS redirect rules
Create mangle rules for policy-based routing with routing marks
Build dynamic address-lists with timeout for DoS blacklisting
Write idempotent firewall scripts using comment-as-tag pattern
Set up interface-lists for managing multiple interfaces with single rules
limitations: Does not execute commands on live RouterOS devices - provides configuration syntax only
Cannot access RouterOS device state or existing configuration
Does not validate against specific RouterOS version capabilities
Does not replace RouterOS API knowledge for actual device management

use_cases.title: Network Administrator Writing Firewall Rules
use_cases.description: Generate correct RouterOS firewall configurations for MikroTik routers without memorizing CLI syntax. Get filter rules, NAT, and address-lists that follow RouterOS best practices.

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Automating Router Configuration
use_cases.description: Use Claude Code to generate idempotent firewall scripts with proper cleanup patterns. Ensure firewall configurations can be re-run safely without creating duplicate rules.

use_cases.title: Developer Adding Firewall Features to Network Apps
use_cases.description: Integrate RouterOS firewall configuration knowledge into applications that manage MikroTik devices. Get accurate syntax for firewall features including DoS protection and policy routing.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers building network management tools

prompt_templates.title: Basic Firewall Rule
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a RouterOS firewall filter rule that accepts SSH connections from 10.0.0.0/24 to the router itself. Use the input chain.

prompt_templates.title: NAT Port Forward
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a NAT rule that forwards external TCP port 8080 to internal server at 192.168.1.100 port 80. The WAN interface is ether1.

prompt_templates.title: DoS Protection with Address Lists
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a three-stage DoS protection pattern using psd detection, connection-limit thresholds, and tarpit action. Include automatic address-list expiration.

prompt_templates.title: Policy Routing with Mangle
prompt_templates.prompt: Write mangle rules for policy-based routing. Mark traffic from 10.20.0.0/24 with routing-mark vpn-mark. Include DNS exemption rules and hot spot authentication filtering.

output_examples.input: Create a firewall rule that blocks all WAN input except established connections
output_examples.output:
Place this rule in the input chain:

/ip/firewall/filter/add chain=input in-interface-list=WAN connection-state=established,related action=accept comment="allow-established-wan"

/ip/firewall/filter/add chain=input in-interface-list=WAN action=drop comment="block-wan-input"

output_examples.input: Set up automatic blacklisting for port scanners using address-lists
output_examples.output:
Detection rule:

/ip/firewall/filter/add chain=input protocol=tcp psd=21,3s,3,1 action=add-src-to-address-list address-list=port-scanners address-list-timeout=1h comment="detect-portscan"

Block rule:

/ip/firewall/filter/add chain=input src-address-list=port-scanners action=drop comment="block-portscanners"

best_practices: Use place-before=0 to insert accept rules BEFORE drop rules - RouterOS evaluates top-to-bottom
Use address-lists instead of individual IP rules to scale firewall configurations
Tag firewall rules with comment prefixes like myapp- for idempotent cleanup scripts
anti_patterns: Do not write one rule per IP address - use src-address-list= or dst-address-list= instead
Do not forget place-before=0 on accept rules - default append places them AFTER drop rules
Do not use remove [find dynamic=no] in cleanup scripts - it removes ALL static rules

faq.question: How do I prevent duplicate rules when re-running firewall scripts?
faq.answer: Use the comment-as-tag pattern. First remove rules with matching comment prefix: /ip/firewall/filter/remove [find comment~"myapp-"], then add rules with the same comment prefix. This ensures idempotent scripts.

faq.question: Why does my accept rule not work even though it is above the drop rule?
faq.answer: RouterOS evaluates rules top-to-bottom with first match winning. Ensure your accept rule uses place-before=0 to insert at position 0. Also check that non-terminal actions like add-src-to-address-list do not have passthrough=yes that continues evaluation.

faq.question: What is the difference between connection-state values in RouterOS?
faq.answer: RouterOS uses new, established, related, invalid, and untracked. The invalid state catches malformed packets. Established and related match return traffic. Untracked matches packets marked via /ip/firewall/raw action=notrack.

faq.question: How do I protect against DoS attacks on a MikroTik router?
faq.answer: Use the three-stage pattern: detect with psd or connection-limit, blacklist with address-lists with timeout, and tarpit or drop blacklisted sources. The skill includes complete examples for port scan detection and connection flood protection.

faq.question: Can I combine FastTrack with mangle-based policy routing?
faq.answer: No. FastTrack bypasses mangle chains entirely. If a connection is fasttracked, mark-routing rules are not applied. Choose either FastTrack for performance or mangle for policy routing, not both on the same traffic.

faq.question: How do interface-lists improve firewall rule management?
faq.answer: Interface-lists group multiple interfaces under one name. Instead of writing separate rules for ether1, ether2, and pppoe-out1, create an interface-list named WAN and use in-interface-list=WAN. Add or remove interfaces from the list without modifying firewall rules.
